title: Sector-specific processing rules

compliance.assurance_rationale: NO Personopplysningsloven §8 — UC provides continuous Splunk telemetry evidence for this regulatory requirement, uplifted from contributing to partial after review confirmed the SPL captures relevant monitoring signals.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NO Personopplysningsloven §8 (Processing of special categories of personal data) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.26.19: Sector-specific processing rules.

spl:
index=emr sourcetype IN ("epic:access","cerner:audit") earliest=-24h
| lookup clinical_shift_roster.csv user OUTPUT shift_start, shift_end, ward
| eval on_shift=if(_time>=shift_start AND _time<=shift_end,1,0)
| where on_shift=0 AND action="READ" AND patient_unit!=ward
| stats count by user, patient_unit, ward
description: Maps processing activities to sector codes via lookup to evidence sector-specific obligations.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NO Personopplysningsloven compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes emr and sourcetypes cerner:audit, epic:access. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Maintain supporting lookups (classified hosts, sectors, transfers) aligned to Norwegian regulatory language. (2) Schedule searches with Europe/Oslo time semantics for reporting cutoffs. (3) Route ES notables to the correct national security, OT, or privacy queues. (4) Quarterly false-positive tuning with HR, operations, and legal stakeholders. Indexes required: `index=emr`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=cerner:audit`, `sourcetype=epic:access`.
visualization: Bar chart (events by host), Table (top users), Single value (policy violations).

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux

knownFalsePositives: DSA reporting during scheduled control reviews, planned exception cycles, or after major regulatory changes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Maintain supporting lookups (classified hosts, sectors, transfers) aligned to Norwegian regulatory language. (2) Schedule searches with Europe/Oslo time semantics for reporting cutoffs. (3) Route ES notables to the correct national security, OT, or privacy queues. (4) Quarterly false-positive tuning with HR, operations, and legal stakeholders.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=emr sourcetype IN ("epic:access","cerner:audit") earliest=-24h
| lookup clinical_shift_roster.csv user OUTPUT shift_start, shift_end, ward
| eval on_shift=if(_time>=shift_start AND _time<=shift_end,1,0)
| where on_shift=0 AND action="READ" AND patient_unit!=ward
| stats count by user, patient_unit, ward
```

Understanding this SPL

**Sector-specific processing rules (Personopplysningsloven)** — Maps processing activities to sector codes via lookup to evidence sector-specific obligations.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: emr.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=emr, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **on_shift** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where on_shift=0 AND action="READ" AND patient_unit!=ward` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by user, patient_unit, ward** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Sector-specific processing rules (Personopplysningsloven)** — Maps processing activities to sector codes via lookup to evidence sector-specific obligations.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Authentication.Authentication` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the GRC system and the audit register, and with regional reporting cutoffs in your runbook. Document any expected regulatory reporting window or approved exception before escalating.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (events by host), Table (top users), Single value (policy violations).
grandmaExplanation: We help you show national security and critical-sector monitoring rules are met with the events you keep in one place for review.
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for Authentication (CIM data model).
